frozen_components: LLM backbone (Deepseek-7B-base or other specified models)
architecture.summary: PQM reframes process reward modeling (PRM) as a Markov Decision Process where a reward model scores intermediate reasoning steps in language model trajectories. The system defines a Q-value function as the probability of achieving a correct final answer from each state-action pair, then trains the reward model to approximate optimal Q-value rankings across steps using a margin-based comparative loss function rather than step-wise classification. Information flows from reasoning trajectories through a value head on top of a frozen LLM backbone, which outputs scalar Q-value estimates for each step; these estimates are optimized to preserve the theoretically-derived ranking relationships among correct and incorrect steps.
trained_components: value head (scalar reward model on top of frozen LLM)

biblio.title: Process Reward Model with Q-Value Rankings

claims.statement: PQM improves the accuracy from 39.8% to 51.4%, a direct 11.6% improvement on the challenging MATH500 benchmark when verifying solutions sampled from the Llama-3-70B-Instruct model
PQM achieves 51.4% accuracy at BON@128 on MATH500 with Llama-3-70B-Instruct sampling policy
PQM achieves 65.25% accuracy at BON@64 on GSM-Plus with MetaMath-Mistral-7B sampling policy
Q-values ascend with the continuation of correct steps and descend as wrong steps proceed, while having a prominent gap between correct and wrong steps (Theorem 3.5)

Classification-based PRM can be cast as a special case of the Q-value ranking framework under the condition that next-step error probability from correct states approaches zero and trajectory success from incorrect states approaches zero (Lemma 3.6)
The margin hyperparameter α in the comparative loss function should be set to values between 2 and 8; extreme values (α=0 or α=16) lead to suboptimal performance

PQM trained with the practical comparative loss (Eq. 10) outperforms the theoretical comparative loss (Eq. 9), indicating that the theoretical loss overemphasizes discrepancies between correct and incorrect steps while neglecting the ascending relationship among correct steps

PQM generalizes across six different LLM backbones (Deepseek-Math-7B-base, Deepseek-Math-7B-rl, Qwen2-Math-1.5B, Qwen2-Math-1.5B-Instruct, Metamath-7B, Metamath-13B) and consistently outperforms BCE-based PRM across all tested configurations
